definition: A glamour is an illusion-spell that deceives the eye — and, faded into fashion, dazzling attractiveness. The two senses are one Scots word.
history: Astonishingly, a second mutation of *grammar* (like *grimoire*): a Scots word for 'a magic spell that deceives the eye,' popularized by Walter Scott around 1830 and softened over a century into 'dazzling attractiveness.' Call a star glamorous and you call them, at root, enchanted. See the full Word History.

etymology.note: Astonishingly, glamour is ALSO a mutation of ‘grammar’ — a second magic-word born from the mystique of book-learning. Fashion-glamour is a 20c. softening of ‘a magic illusion.’

essay.title: Glamour & grimoire: two spells from one grammar
essay.dek: A fashion word, a spell-book, and a dictionary — all the same word.
essay.html:
Here is the best trick in the whole magical lexicon, and it is entirely true. Take the dry word grammar — Greek grammatikē, 'the art of letters.' In the Middle Ages, when nearly all serious learning was Latin and looked, to the unlettered, indistinguishable from sorcery, 'grammar' picked up a second life meaning occult learning. A book of that learning became a grimoire — French pronouncing 'grammar' badly, and the mispronunciation stuck to the spell-book. (See the full grimoire history.)

But there is a third child. In Scots, the same 'grammar' mutated into glamour — 'a magic spell, an enchantment that deceives the eye.' To 'cast the glamour' was to bewitch someone into seeing what wasn't there. Walter Scott put it into wide circulation in the early 1800s; from there it drifted, over a century, into 'dazzling, illusory attractiveness.' Call a film star glamorous and you call them, at the root, enchanted.

Grammar, grimoire, glamour: a dictionary, a spell-book, and a red-carpet adjective, all one syllable, all descended from the ancient suspicion that people who can read are dangerous. It's the thesis of this whole project — that the words for magic carry, fossilised inside them, whole attitudes about knowledge and power.

sources.label: Etymonline / OED, s.vv. glamour, grammar, grimoire
Walter Scott, Letters on Demonology and Witchcraft (popularises 'glamour')

flavor_snippets.html: Protected by <mark>glamers</mark> and guile, Glen Elendra harbors the elusive Oona, queen of the fae.
Many peasants secretly admire vampires' <mark>glamour</mark> and elegance—until they witness a moment of pure bloodlust.
One <mark>glamer</mark> leads him far from home. The next washes away his memory that home was ever anywhere but at her side.
Merrow <mark>glamers</mark> have disarmed many a foe when a shield has failed.
He weaves <mark>glamours</mark> as easily as breathing, his otherworldly beauty entrancing fae and mortal alike.

"All <mark>glamers</mark> lifted, all lies revealed, all flesh exposed."
—Awylla, elvish safewright
Even treefolk witches steer clear of the unpredictable woods near Glen Elendra. Spells refract off the <mark>glamer</mark>-polished trees and the eyes of lurking pucas.
A well-aged boggart body is steeped in magics richer than any fae <mark>glamer</mark>.
A delicate veil of <mark>glamers</mark> can prove as sturdy as iron.
